title: Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects
Identify key diagnostic features of Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects
Apply clinical reasoning to Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects management
Recognize common pitfalls in Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects

sections.title: Overview of Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects
sections.content:
**Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects**

Septum primum/secundum formation, endocardial cushion defects, VSD types, hemodynamic consequences

**Key Points:**
• ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome)
• VSD: Most common congenital heart defect; Membranous (most common type), Muscular
• Small VSD: loud holosystolic murmur, often closes spontaneously

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome)
• VSD: Most common congenital heart defect; Membranous (most common type), Muscular

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Small VSD: loud holosystolic murmur, often closes spontaneously
• Large VSD: heart failure, failure to thrive, pulmonary overcirculation
• Eisenmenger syndrome: chronic left-to-right shunt → pulmonary HTN → shunt reversal (cyanosis)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects:**

• Small VSD: loud holosystolic murmur, often closes spontaneously
• Large VSD: heart failure, failure to thrive, pulmonary overcirculation
• Eisenmenger syndrome: chronic left-to-right shunt → pulmonary HTN → shunt reversal (cyanosis)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Atrial & Ventricular Septal Defects?

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases
ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome)
options.explanation: ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome)

correct.reinforcement: Correct! ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome).
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome)

coachDx.misconception: While typically self-resolving without treatment may seem plausible, it does not match the clinical scenario described.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum  from Typically self-resolving without treatment.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion.
